Frontier-lab infrastructure, now as a service
Fireworks AI
Fireworks AI
Run AI faster, more efficiently, and on your own terms
Published Jun 24, 2026
+ Follow

The hard part of reinforcement learning on a frontier model is the infrastructure that keeps training and inference numerically identical: zero KLD, end to end. We've solved this challenge, and are now offering it as a managed service, starting with GLM 5.2.

For years, the club of teams getting reinforcement learning to actually work on giant models was limited to the top frontier labs. Not because they had better algorithms, but because they had the unglamorous infrastructure underneath: training and serving stacks engineered to produce the same numbers, run after run, request after request.

That infrastructure is built on concepts people outside those labs have never had to think about, for example batch invariance and zero-KLD across training and serving: making the rollout engine and trainer produce the same numbers end to end. It sounds like plumbing, but it's the difference between an RL run that hill climbs successfully, and one that quietly falls apart.

The toolkit that used to be locked inside a handful of labs is now available as a managed service on Fireworks on GLM 5.2, the OSS model sitting at the very top of independent community leaderboards. We are offering frontier specialized intelligence as a service: not a frontier you rent prompts from, but one you can post-train and reinforce on the same infrastructure the top labs built in-house.

The features that used to be frontier-lab-only

Reinforcement learning on an LLM is a loop: the model generates responses, those responses are scored, and the trainer nudges the weights. The whole thing rests on the assumption that the probability the trainer thinks the model gave each token matches the probability the serving engine actually used to generate it. When that holds, learning signal flows. When it doesn't, you're optimizing against noise.

Holding that assumption on a modern frontier model is genuinely hard, and Fireworks gives you the tools the big labs built to do it:

Batch invariance for Large MoEs - a request returns the same result no matter what other traffic happens to share its batch. Without it, an "on-policy" run is quietly off-policy: the rollout was generated by a subtly different model than the one you're updating, just because the server was busy. The building blocks are increasingly public: open-source engines ship batch-invariant kernels for smaller, dense models (
Zero-KLD train/serve alignment - for a model like GLM 5.2, the usual patches don't reach far enough. The popular Mixture-of-Experts fix, router replay (replaying the serving engine's expert choices inside the trainer), handles which experts fire but it can't touch the other place these models diverge: which tokens the sparse-attention indexer selects. Those selections aren't tractable to replay. So there's no halfway house here. The generation engine, the prompt-reading path, and the trainer have to share one numerical definition, so what you train is exactly what you served: zero KLD, end to end.

These are exactly the pieces most platforms don't have, and their absence is why so many RL efforts stall.

Validations

Here is the same RL task, the GLM countdown reasoning task, run two ways. Both use the same algorithm and data. The only difference is the numerics underneath.

Without the Fireworks numerics stack, the trainer and the rollout engine disagree (train-inference KL around 0.013), and the run leans hard on the industry's usual crutch: importance sampling and clipping were discarding about 45% of every batch's tokens just to stay upright. It still wasn't enough. Around step 20 the reward collapses, falling from around 0.9 to under 0.2 as the policy chases a target that no longer matches what it generated.

With the Fireworks stack, the trainer and serving engine run at zero KLD, end to end - bit-for-bit identical - with zero tokens clipped, and reward stays healthy across the entire run. Same task, same algorithm. The only thing that changed was making the numbers agree.

That's the trap with the importance-sampling-and-clipping approach: it's a tax, not a fix. Every clipped token is learning signal thrown away, and past a point no amount of clipping saves a run whose numbers don't line up.

How we did it, and why it's hard

It comes down to a property of floating-point math that trips up almost everyone: addition isn't associative. (a + b) + c doesn't equal a + (b + c) down at the bit level, so the order in which a GPU adds numbers up changes the answer, usually in the last few digits, occasionally by enough to flip a token.

A frontier MoE changes that order constantly, for reasons that have nothing to do with your request:

Latent attention (MLA) - the compression trick that makes GLM cheap at long context splits its reduction into chunks across the GPU whose boundaries shift with whatever sequence lengths happen to share the batch, so the same query can accumulate its attention in a different order from one moment to the next.
The sparse indexer that decides which past tokens each query even looks at can hand back the same set in a different order. Because the attention sum follows that order, the result drifts.
Each expert's matmul runs a different kernel and tiling depending on how many tokens that expert drew this step, which depends on everyone else's tokens, not just yours.
The router can land on a near-tie between two experts; a rounding-error-sized wobble flips which one fires, and the token's entire computation changes with it.
Across GPUs, the all-reduce that stitches partial sums back together switches algorithms by message size, which again rides on load.

Stack those together and "temperature 0" on a busy server is quietly nondeterministic: the same prompt, co-batched with different traffic, comes back subtly and sometimes meaningfully different. That's the gap that silently turns an on-policy RL run off-policy.

Getting to numerics you can trust meant pinning every one of those decisions so it depends only on your request: a fixed reduction order in attention no matter the batch, one settled kernel choice for the expert matmuls regardless of token counts, a deterministic tie-break in the router, and a single fixed cross-GPU reduction path, all without giving up so much speed that the trainer becomes unusable for everyday SFT/DPO.

We then aligned the trainer's own forward pass to that same serving definition so on the validated GLM-5.2 LoRA path, trainer and serving reach zero KLD - a train-inference generation KL of exactly 0, bit-for-bit identical, and the served model returns the same output at temperature 0 regardless of concurrency.

Zero KLD, end to end

Tiny train-inference numerical disagreements aren't benign noise: they quietly turn on-policy RL off-policy and can independently cause a run to collapse. For related background on inference nondeterminism, see TML; for the RL failure mode, see this diagnosis. The clean answer isn't to correct the gap with importance sampling, which only piles on variance; it's to erase it. When training and inference are made bitwise consistent so the KLD is exactly 0, RL trains in fewer steps and reaches higher reward (vLLM x TorchTitan).

That's what Fireworks delivers on GLM 5.2: batch-invariant serving with zero-KLD train-inference alignment. The rollout engine returns bit-for-bit identical logits no matter the batch size, the concurrent load, or how many GPUs it's sharded across, and the trainer is held to that same bit-exact standard, so the full train-rollout loop runs at zero KLD. This is the guarantee frontier labs build in-house, and it's genuinely hard to hold across different engines, kernels, and parallelism layouts. Determinism across tensor-parallel sizes alone was an open research problem as recently as this year (TBIK, ICML 2026).

The catch everyone else hits is speed: open-source deterministic modes typically run 35-60% slower (SGLang). Fireworks pays virtually none of that tax. The GLM trainer holds around 3,500 tokens/sec per node, on par with the OSS TileLang implementation, and layers the zero-KLD numerics on top rather than trading speed for them. One stack gives the SFT/DPO majority full throughput and gives RL teams zero-KLD numerics: frontier-lab infrastructure, delivered as a managed service.

What you get on Fireworks today

A frontier model, RL-ready. GLM 5.2 is live for fine-tuning through the Fireworks Training API, with the full numerical foundation carried forward from GLM 5.1. The validated training shape is public today.
The methods that matter. SFT, DPO, and RL through the Training API; SFT and DPO on managed training. On-policy RL where the trainer and serving engine genuinely agree so your signal is real learning, not drift you clipped away.
Reproducible, auditable inference. Temperature-0 requests return the same answer regardless of server load so you get trustworthy evals, meaningful regression tests, and the reproducibility enterprise compliance demands.
Fast where it counts. A trainer tuned for SFT/DPO throughput, rollouts that generate about 1.8x faster on GLM 5.2 than GLM 5.1 (around 5,000 tokens/sec per node, per promotion-CI), and zero-KLD train-serve numerics, so RL is genuinely on-policy.
Managed, self-service, and co-located. Run the full loop on managed infrastructure with trainer and deployment co-located for fast weight sync, or drive longer runs yourself through the API.

Why it matters: frontier specialized intelligence as a service

The frontier is open now. Anyone can download a state-of-the-art model. What's still scarce is the ability to do reinforcement learning on one of these models correctly: a loop whose numbers line up well enough to converge, results you can reproduce, and a trainer fast enough to iterate on. That used to live only inside the biggest labs.

That's the work behind GLM 5.1 and GLM 5.2 on Fireworks: bitwise zero-KLD numerics for RL and raw speed for SFT/DPO, in one stack. It's exactly the infrastructure that used to require a frontier lab's in-house systems team, now available as a managed service with Fireworks. Frontier weights are the raw material. Numerics you can trust - batch invariance and zero-KLD train/serve consistency - are what turn them into your model.

In the spirit of the legendary NFL coach Vince Lombardi:

"Practice does not make perfect. Only perfect practice makes perfect."

That's the essence of frontier specialized intelligence as a service: a perfect RL training system which empowers AI natives and modern enterprises to own their AI future.

To get started, please reach out to our training team today, or dive straight in with our managed training docs.

.

Claude is DEAD. We just replaced our $113K anthropic invoice with an open source model that cost 80% less and performs exactly the same. This changes everything we know about the AI ecosystem.

ok ok, ofc Claude is not dead (cheap shot i know). it takes more than an open source release to kill the $50B ARR juggernaut.

what died is the reflex.

the reflex to send every task to the smartest model in the room. here me out....

At Swan, we had it bad. Our AI agent ran on a mix of Sonnet + Opus (anthropic's most powerful models), and for the last 3 months - we've been hitting the $100K monthly invoices like it's nothing.

we watched the invoice climb past $100K again and again, and told ourselves that was the price of frontier quality.

then Minimax dropped last week, GLM right behind it. the buzz around these models was hard to ignore.

so we ran the evals expecting a gap.

there wasn't one. not for the work that actually fills our days.. scoring leads, researching prospects, drafting follow-ups, summarizing calls.

80% cheaper. same output on the boring 90% that makes up most of any agent's day.

The open source community just proved you can get frontier quality at wholesale price.

Don't get me wrong. Claude still wins the hard stuff. it always did. the mistake was paying frontier prices for everything else.

so we're doing the obvious thing.. pushing it straight into the Swan AI product.

every dollar we stop burning on the boring 90% is a dollar that goes back to the people using swan every day.. in margin, in speed, in more agent for the same spend.

crazy times!

wdyt? how will this affect Anthropic ahead of it's IPO???

The harness caveat is the part people keep missing. We have quietly entered an era where model quality and harness fit are two different axes. A model can be genuinely excellent on its own and still feel mediocre inside a coding agent that was shaped around a specific model's quirks. It makes raw open weights more interesting than benchmarks suggest, because the ceiling moves once the scaffolding is tuned for it rather than against it. Feels like the real competition is shifting from the model alone to the model plus the loop you wrap around it.

This matches exactly what I keep running into: the open versus closed gap people quote from leaderboards is mostly a harness gap, not a model gap. When I lined up GLM-5.2 against Opus, the reasoning scores were a tie or a GLM win, but the agentic numbers (Tool-Decathlon, SWE-bench Pro) favored the closed model, and a lot of that is the proprietary harness being tuned around its own model's quirks. A model-agnostic harness like dcode is the right place to test that fairly, otherwise you are benchmarking the scaffolding, not the model. Curious how much of the gap you see close once GLM-5.2 runs in a harness built for it rather than against it?

Agreed, and the model-agnostic part matters more than people expect, because the harness itself is a variable in any model comparison. The last time we swapped only the model under the same agent loop, most of the weaker model's failures were tool-call format mismatches rather than worse reasoning, since the prompt and parsing were tuned to the original model. A fair GLM 5.2 read depends on whether the harness adapts the tool interface per model or forces every model into one convention.
 
Does dcode normalize the tool-calling format per provider, or is there still a single prompt template the model has to fit?

A quiet shout-out to Fireworks AI. We've been running serious inference volume through their serverless infrastructure, and it's been excellent: few errors, consistent throughput, predictable latency, fair pricing. In a space where flaky serving has become the norm, that consistency is appreciated.

The chart below shows one of their smartest plays: dynamic rate limits. Watch the dotted line. When our usage spiked, the limit jumped sharply with it, giving us headroom right when we needed it instead of throttling us mid-burst. Then as our load tapered off over the following days, the limit stepped back down; gradually, predictably, never yanked out from under us. It rewards consistent usage with more capacity and reclaims it fairly when you go quiet. No rigid one-size-fits-all tiers that surprise both the vendor and the user; just limits that track how you actually behave.

Contrast that with vendors who push you into big fixed tiers where spiky usage still wrecks their load planning and your reliability. Fireworks aligned their infrastructure with real usage patterns, and everyone wins. Pragmatic engineering over pricing-page theater.

Chris Qin
1w

The next AI battle isn't about intelligence.
It's about control.

For three years, the industry has competed on one axis: who has the smartest model. Who tops MMLU, SWE-bench, GPQA. Who reasons better, codes better, plans better.

In the last two quarters, the enterprise conversation has shifted. I've talked to a handful of Fortune 500 CTOs recently, and they were all circling the same question:

"Who controls the stack?"
Not because intelligence stopped mattering. Because intelligence is starting to look like a commodity. New open-weight models — Qwen, DeepSeek, Llama, Mistral — close the gap with frontier APIs every few months. And the moment that gap closes, the real question stops being "which model is smartest" and becomes "who controls how, where, and at what cost it runs."

That's where the next moat is forming.
Control over which model serves which request — and the freedom to swap it next quarter without rewriting the app.
Control over which GPU runs it — H100s today, H200s next quarter, a mix of consumer cards for batch jobs, whatever the supply chain (or the procurement team) hands you.
Control over where inference happens. Some workloads stay in your VPC. Some need to sit on-prem next to the data. Some can burst to public cloud. Most enterprises end up needing all three, often in the same week.

And control over cost. The teams winning right now aren't the ones paying the lowest per-token price — they're the ones who can route between models and hardware as prices, latencies, and SLAs move.

In practice, the teams moving fastest tend to share a pattern. They treat inference as infrastructure, not as a vendor relationship. They pick an inference layer they actually own, and they make sure the same layer runs across public cloud, customer VPCs, and on-prem H100s/H200s — without three different rewrites.

Teams that build this way absorb every new model release as an upgrade. Teams that don't get repriced — by the vendor, by the regulator, or by the next open-weight launch.

Intelligence is becoming a commodity. Control is becoming the moat.
That's the thesis we're building Xinference on — a portable inference layer that keeps the choice of model, GPU, and deployment in your hands.

We take the scars so you get the zero-touch pipeline. To everyone else fighting the cloud today to build something real: the finish line is worth it. Keep pushing. 🚀

Tony Costanzo

Most companies treat AI like electricity now. You rent it, it shows up, you never think about where it comes from.

But every time I sit with a construction or engineering team, the same question surfaces.. usually quietly: "to use this, do I have to ship my contracts and project data to someone else's servers?"

For a lot of the work I do, that isn't a small question.
It's the whole question.
So I spent real time on the alternative: how far can you actually get running AI on your own hardware, in your own building, paying no subscription?

I tested two open models from the same family on a pair of consumer GPUs.. the kind of thing you could put under a desk, not in a data center.
One was the big "flagship" with the better benchmark scores. The other was a leaner Mixture-of-Experts model: larger overall, but only a sliver of it runs at any moment, so it's far cheaper to run.

I expected the flagship to clearly win. It edged it on the benchmark, by four points.
Then I watched them do real work:
- the leaner model answered 5.5x faster on long documents
- it held 2.5x more context on the same hardware
- it tied the flagship on every hard reasoning and code task I gave it by hand

Here's the honest part:
Local AI isn't a toy anymore. It also isn't a clean replacement for the frontier subscriptions, and I won't pretend it is.
It sits somewhere more interesting.

For a huge slice of real work.. reading documents, answering questions against your own data, drafting, routine reasoning.. a model running in your building is now fast enough, capable enough, and private by default.
The catch was never the model. It's that we defaulted to renting before anyone checked what we already had the hardware to own.

That's the part worth exploring. Not "local vs cloud," but what's actually worth keeping inside the building.
Full writeup, real numbers, and the setup details in the comments.

Shrey Desai
3d Edited

The Fable 5 Problem

The Fable 5 model shutdown exposed two risks in how most teams deploy AI. The first is architectural: your system depends on one model, it goes down, and everything stops. The second is ownership: you don't control the weights, so someone else decides when you lose access.

On (1): Ensemble methods like boosting/stacking have combined weaker models to outperform any single one since the mid-1990s. What's compelling is that orchestration is now matching or beating frontier solo models on hard benchmarks, e.g. Sakana Fugu (https://sakana.ai/fugu/) scores 73.7 on SWE-Bench Pro (reportedly on-par with Fable 5 / Mythos Preview) by dynamically routing tasks across specialist agents. But multi-model setups are not bulletproof: they pay a latency/throughput tax (optimizations like KV caching and speculative decoding don't transfer across model boundaries) and orchestrating across multiple closed models doesn't fix the deeper risk. Diversifying across closed providers is still "renting".

On (2): Open-weight models are quickly closing the quality gap with closed frontier models, e.g. on FrontierSWE, GLM-5.2 only trails Claude Opus 4.8 by 1%, while edging out GPT-5.5 by 1% (https://z.ai/blog/glm-5.2). Open models also retain serving optimizations that multi-model setups forfeit: KV caching and speculative decoding all require tight coupling within a single model family. And -- like Bitcoin -- once weights are released, no single government can recall them. You can self-host, fork, fine-tune, switch infrastructure providers, etc without asking permission. 

Where does this go? Lin Qiao put it simply: "a company that owns its intelligence gets better every week". That principle extends to any developer, lab, or government that controls its own weights. We now have two levers to hedge against supply-side risk -- multi-model orchestration for redundancy, and open weights for sovereignty -- and the combination of both opens new ground for building agentic systems.

Gary Fuller

What happens when the model you built on disappears.

Fable and Mythos just got shut down by government action. Not deprecated, not sunset with a migration path. Shut down. Anyone who wired those into a production workflow woke up to broken systems and no recourse. An unfortunate reality of using rented AI models in your workflow. When the model lives behind someone else's API, you are renting capability you do not control, and the lease can be cancelled by a regulator, a policy change, or a quarterly business decision.

I run a hybrid stack precisely because of this. Most work does not need a frontier model. Drafting, summarization, classification, code completion, the long tail of routine tasks all run fine on local models I own outright. On Apple Silicon and comparable hardware the unified memory makes this practical today, not in some future roadmap. The data never leaves the machine, the marginal cost per token is effectively zero, and the latency is better because there is no network round trip. For the narrow slice of work that genuinely needs the frontier, I route out to the API and pay for it deliberately.

That is the architecture I think most serious teams land on. Not local instead of frontier. Local for the eighty percent, frontier for the twenty that requires it.

The framing that matters is sovereignty. Owned weights cannot be shut down. They cannot be repriced overnight. They do not vanish because a vendor changed terms or a government changed its mind. You can audit them, pin a version, and run them in five years exactly as they run today.

Open weight models keep closing the gap with the frontier tier, which means the slice that requires a rented model keeps shrinking. Build so that when the next API goes dark, and one will, the lights stay on.

evidence.author: FireworksAI_HQ
evidence.text:
We just open-sourced Fireworks Manus, a powerful browser-based agent built using DeepSeek V3 for reasoning and FireLlava 13B for visual understanding.

This agent doesn’t just generate text. It:

→ Navigates the web
→ Clicks buttons, fills out forms
→ Extracts dynamic content
→ Handles auth flows, modals, and even CAPTCHAs

It’s powered by Fireworks AI’s fast, multimodal inference stack, making it responsive enough to keep up with modern interactive websites.

We designed it with a structured agent architecture:

👁️ Vision system - DOM, screenshots, and spatial awareness
🧠 Reasoning system - memory, goal tracking, JSON-mode planning
🖱️ Action system - full control over browser interactions

The result: a robust Observe → Decide → Act loop that adapts to real-world UX in real time.

This is the future of agentic automation - no brittle scripts, just intelligent, web-native agents.

📹 Watch the demo for the tool: https://t.co/TFmEM0LmfD

⚒️ Full repo, docs, and how to run it: https://t.co/nDQ3xi92lv

💻 Detailed blog about Fireworks Manus: https://t.co/pwwWJ7EdHB

evidence.text:
🔥 Launching the Fireworks AI Experimentation Platform (GA) and Build SDK (Beta)🔥

Top AI teams don't win by chance - they win by running 10X more experiments than competitors.

Launching early, collecting feedback, tuning and selecting models, running offline evals, and validating impact with product metrics in a fast, continuous cycle is critical to build successful AI products.

But, the need to manage complex infrastructure, wait for GPUs, reconcile differences between training and serving, and maintain reliability, slow AI teams down.

Now, with the Firework’s experimentation platform, experimenting with open models just got a whole lot easier. With the help of our brand-new Python SDK (in beta), you can:

→ Stop waiting for GPUs, fine-tune and deploy 1000s of models on a single GPU deployment using Multi-LoRA

→ Programmatically run large-scale fine-tuning experiments

→ Scale evals with fully managed and autoscaling GPU deployments

Check out our blog post to learn more and see a demo of running 125 experiments in 3 minutes on a single deployment: https://t.co/MmNfki7n9g

evidence.rewritten_text: The author says the GIL has been in Python since the beginning, ensuring only one Python instruction runs at a time, which is convenient and safe because Python cannot crash from memory corruption due to a data race like C++ often can. They also say pure Python gets barely faster with threads.
evidence.text: GIL has been in Python from the beginning. It ensures that only a single Python instruction can execute at any given time. Very convenient and safe - Python just can’t crash on memory corruption from a data race like C++ often does. But pure Python gets barely faster with threads

- Judge score: 0.568 vs. 0.521 and 0.466
- Time per task: 321s vs. 775s and 392s
- Cost per task: $0.92 vs. $1.76 and $2.06

Most importantly, Faros tested the models on its own repositories and work, not just public benchmarks.

Model choice should be based on where a model clears your bar, on your work, at a cost that makes sense.

evidence.author: lqiao
evidence.text:
🚀 MCP Support with Fireworks Responses API

If you’ve tried wiring an LLM-powered agent into real systems, you already know the drill:

- Endless boilerplate for calling tools and parsing tool-returns
- Error-handling and retries that sprawl across your codebase
- Latency headaches from round-tripping between model and tools

We felt that pain too, so we moved the whole agent loop server-side. 

✅ One endpoint – fire a prompt, get structured results back
✅ Model Context Protocol out of the box – describe any tool once, we handle the selection & execution
✅ OpenAI-compatible – drop-in for your existing response API calls, no extra overhead for your development flow

What can you build faster with @FireworksAI_HQ ? 

- 🛒 E-commerce agents that check inventory or process returns
- 📋 Ops bots that hit internal HR / PM APIs
- 📊 Data agents that query warehouses and return SQL results straight to the chat

Spend time on product logic, not plumbing.

evidence.author: FireworksAI_HQ
evidence.text:
Qwen 3 is now live on Fireworks AI!

Qwen 3 is a SOTA open-weights model for building AI agents:

➡️ SOTA agentic tool use: Highest ranked open model on BFCL (tool-calling) benchmark, below gpt4-2024-11-20 and above gpt4.5. Tuned to support Model Context Protocol (MCP) tools with the Qwen-Agent framework for multi-step tool usage (function calling), browser actions, and code execution. Fireworks support function calling for Qwen 3 models.

➡️ Top-tier coding and reasoning capabilities: Outperforms many open and closed models on LiveCodeBench, and only three thousands coders on CodeForces are better at competitive coding than Qwen3.

➡️ Hybrid reasoning with enable_thinking: Qwen 3 introduces a dynamic soft switch. You can control whether the model reasons step-by-step (/think) or responds instantly (/no_think), even within the same conversation.

➡️ Massive long-context capabilities: Native 32K token context window (with verified support beyond 130K tokens), perfect for full codebases, research archives, and large chat histories.

➡️True multilingual reach: Trained on 36 trillion tokens across 119 languages and dialects, which delivers strong multilingual instruction-following and generation.

➡️MoE efficiency advantage: Activates only a fraction of experts per token, delivering strong reasoning quality with significantly lower compute and memory requirements compared to dense models of similar total size.

➡️ Fully open-weight under Apache 2.0 license: Fine-tune, customize, and deploy freely with no restrictions.

👉 Try Qwen 3 now on the Fireworks Model Playground https://t.co/dMAPrSrioy

👉 Or integrate it directly via our OpenAI-compatible API

Whether you’re building enterprise copilots, agentic systems, or next-gen search, Qwen 3 gives you unprecedented control over thinking depth, speed, and cost.

evidence.author: dzhulgakov
evidence.rewritten_text: The author thinks diffusion for text is exciting and explains that it enables super-fast generation on GPUs, where Groq and Cerebras are at a disadvantage. They say the approach is slightly worse overall than DeepSeek V2 Lite but much better at fill-in-the-middle, and they hope @InceptionAILabs open sources or publishes the internals.
evidence.text:
Diffusion... for text, wow 🤯. Here's what it means:

1/ Super-speedy generation on GPUs. Groq/Cerebras are at a disadvantage here. Diffusion models (just like LLM training) are all about FLOPs, great for GPUs. Regular LLM inference is more about memory b/w and needs a lot of tricks to run efficiently.

2/ Different strengths and weaknesses compared from other text models. E.g. overall performance is slightly worse than DeepSeek V2 Lite. But it does much better at "fill-in-the-middle" (FIM) compared to other models. FIM is similar to in-painting for images that Diffusion works great for. Maybe it is time for "vibe-based" edits, where the entire text gets morphed at once, conditioned on the prompt.

What does "diffusion" mean? All current LLMs are "autoregressive", i.e. they generate tokens one at a time left to right. Image/video generation models work differently using "diffusion": they start with noise and iteratively replace the entire image. Here they figured out how to make it work for text.

Btw, people often contrast "diffusion" and "transformer". It's really "autoregressive" vs "diffusion" task formulation. Both use "transformer" or could use another architecture.

Funny enough, there's a body of research in the opposite direction: auto-regressive image generation (i.e. in one pass left-to-right, top-to-bottom).

It's very early, but exciting for where this direction may go, and hope @InceptionAILabs open sources or publishes internals!

evidence.author: FireworksAI_HQ
evidence.text:
Everyone has evals. No one knows what to do with them. Meanwhile, your agents aren’t actually getting smarter. Most teams end up with a glorified report card.

But with Eval Protocol, those same evals can finally do real work.
The exact evaluation definition you already have becomes a training signal rather than a static score.
That one eval now powers:
 🚀 Continuous prompt tuning via GEPA
 🚀 Reinforcement fine-tuning
 🚀 Zero extra reward models
 🚀 Zero duplicated metrics or parallel eval stacks

We ran this flow on a real Text2SQL agent:
GEPA alone boosted accuracy from 27% → 43%! Same model, same data, same eval. Then we plugged that same evaluator into Fireworks Reinforcement Fine-Tuning and pushed performance even further. By layering GEPA before RFT, we achieved these gains at a fraction of the cost of pure fine-tuning.

🚀Define your eval once.
🚀Use it everywhere.
🚀Let your agents continuously improve.
If you’re already running evals, you’re way closer than you think.
https://t.co/pdgdgyditJ

evidence.text:
🦙 Exciting news from @Meta -the llama-4 models have just launched, and @FireworksAI_HQ is proud to support on our platform!

Here’s what’s new and noteworthy:

→ A New Herd of Multimodal Models: 
Llama-4 Scout and Maverick integrate text and vision processing for comprehensive understanding across modalities. 

→ Outstanding Performance: 
Llama-4 Scout delivers superior results while requiring only a single H100 GPU, with support for an industry-leading 10M token context window

→ Mixture of Experts (MoE): 
For the first time in Meta’s lineup, Llama-4 models use an MoE architecture, activating only a fraction of parameters per token for higher efficiency and quality.

→ Model Versions:
- Llama-4 Maverick: 17B active parameters, 128 experts, and 400B total parameters, delivering high accuracy at lower cost than Llama 3.3 70B—ideal for advanced image and text tasks in 12 languages.

- Llama-4 Scout: 17B active parameters, 16 experts, and 109B total parameters, now with an enormous 10M-token context window. Perfect for summarizing large documents, reasoning over massive codebases, and building personalized AI-driven tools.

⚒️ Each model features robust safeguards outlined in Meta’s Developer Use Guide. From pre-training to system-level mitigations, Llama-4 helps developers create responsible and adaptive AI applications.

With Llama-4’s powerful multimodal capabilities, massive context windows, and cutting-edge MoE architecture, we can’t wait to see what you build.

Head over to Fireworks AI to get started with the brand-new Llama-4!

🔥 llama4 - maverick: https://t.co/JfuF6cc1u0

evidence.author: dzhulgakov
evidence.rewritten_text: The author says Character AI’s model and infrastructure co-design for efficiency is impressive and notes Fireworks has seen similar wins: MQA instead of GQA, shared KV across layers, sliding windows on some layers, aggressive stateful KV caching with sticky session routing, and full int8 inference including activations and compute. They argue open-source models should be co-designed for inference efficiency, say DeepSeek V2 with MLA is computationally similar to MQA, and ask Meta and Mistral to make native 8-bit models with shrunk KV caches.
evidence.text:
Really impressive model and infra co-design for efficiency from @character_ai (congrats @NoamShazeer @myleott and the team). We've seen similar great wins from some of them at @FireworksAI_HQ:

1) Many tricks to shrink KV cache: MQA (instead of even GQA), share KV across layers, sliding window on some layers. Our earlier explainer on why MQA rocks: https://t.co/M7gsk0tidm .

2) Aggressive stateful KV caching, including "sticky" routing of sessions to the same server. On Fireworks, we commonly see up to 90% hit rates with a similar approach. See the demo from last year on why it matters: https://t.co/7hytpYHrAl

3) full int8 inference - not just weights but activations and compute (also training 🤯). 8bit compute has 2x throughput (same as fp8 results https://t.co/mwbX9fJGOR). Major additional benefit: int math is numerically stable = consistent results across GPUs. It's nearly impossible for floats, e.g. see https://t.co/GoaiV83Yac

Overall, we need more open-source models co-designed with inference efficiency in mind. @deepseek_ai's DeepSeek V2 with MLA is computationally similar to MQA.

@AIatMeta @MistralAI - time for native 8bit models with shrunk KV caches?

evidence.author: FireworksAI_HQ
evidence.text:
AI agents have a scaling problem.
(And we're finally fixing it)

Over the last few years, we saw a pattern:
 → Developers can prototype AI workflows quickly.
 → But moving from prototype to production? Painful.

Most frameworks demand deep AI expertise, heavy Python use, and tangled abstractions.

So we built something different.

💜 Introducing AIML(AI Markup Language)- a prompt-based language and runtime that lets any developer ship enterprise-grade AI agents.

No AI PhD required. No massive SDK. No complex setup.
Just structured prompts that run like reliable programs.

⚙️ AIML works by:
→ Building on Markdown + XML syntax devs already use
→ Running agents in doc-order for predictability
→ Supporting logic, state, and tool integration
→ Working with any LLM provider (like Fireworks AI)
And it’s not just theoretical.

We’ve open-sourced the AIML proxy server.
We’re shipping rich debugging tools: logging, visual flows, live monitoring.
We’re enabling memory and context control, when and how you want.

🔥 More importantly:
→ AIML unifies the "workflow vs agent" debate.
→ It works across languages and teams.
→ It keeps your AI logic version-controlled, testable, and deployable.

All from inside your prompt.

You write once. It runs anywhere. And your agents scale without rewrites.
This is the next step in democratizing AI development.

We’re excited to share it, and we’d love your feedback 💜 

💻 Try it now → https://t.co/vVIgvDuUPv

evidence.author: dzhulgakov
evidence.rewritten_text: The author argues that LLM inference benchmarks should use much longer prompts than the common 3:1 prompt-to-completion ratio, because real-world workloads are often 10:1 or even 30:1 and the speed and cost differences can be dramatic. They say RAG, long chat history, summarization, and short completions make long prompts common, and that tuning deployments for specific prompt and completion lengths can produce major speedups, including 3x higher throughput and lower latency on the same hardware.
evidence.text:
When comparing the speed of LLMs inference, how long should the prompts and completions be?

Current leaderboards from @ArtificialAnlys or @withmartian use 3:1 prompt:completion ratio. But real-world use cases are 10:1 or even 30:1. Speed and cost differences can be dramatic!
I pulled a sample of different use cases served by @FireworksAI_HQ. The most common is 8-10x more tokens in prompts than in completion. Some use cases get into 20-30x territory. Why is it?

The primary source is RAG - i.e. grounding LLMs in external knowledge or context. Extensive RAG can easily have prompts in thousands or tens of thousands of tokens. Other use cases include:

* Lengthy multi-turn chats with long conversation history
* Summarization tasks where input text is naturally long
* Classification or inline code completion where completion is very short.

How do longer prompts affect inference speed? Processing the prompt is 5-50x faster per token than generating completion because it can be done in parallel. Still, with long prompts, this processing stops being negligible. Caching common prompts across requests or allocating dedicated compute to prompt processing greatly helps.

Long prompts make completion slower too. LLMs have 2 major compute blocks: FFN and Attention. FFN cost is the same per token. Attention is ~quadratic in the sequence length and requires linear memory to store the KV cache.
The magnitude of these effects depends significantly on the model architecture and hardware. As we point out in https://t.co/jLyOkVO9bl there’s no one size fit all. We’ve seen multiple X speed-ups from tuning dedicated deployments to specific prompt & completion lengths. Consider this example where a special deployment configuration for long prompt leads to 3X higher throughput and lower latencies than the default short-prompt one (all on the same hardware).

At @FireworksAI_HQ we can help you get the best speed and cost for your unique workload. Reach out to us about your LLM needs.

We’re working with the community to ensure the benchmarks capture a realistic picture. Having workloads with very long prompts is one of the critical factors.

Coding agents break when models are "almost" bug-free. But almost valid JSON is just not the same valid JSON. Fun piece here from @akshay_pachaar shows why SFT can't fix this, and how GRPO trains against correctness directly.  

Worth noting: the reason this works is inference staying in sync with every weight update. That syncing process is what makes RL powerful at scale.

evidence.text:
We ran 720 browser agent tasks with @nottecore across frontier models. 

One baseline model produced malformed outputs in ~1 out of every 5 calls, leading to retries inside multi-step workflows. 

Across Kimi K2.5, GLM-5, and MiniMax M2.5 served on Fireworks, retry rates were near zero and latency stayed stable even as tasks extended across multiple steps.

Same workload. Same agent loop. Different execution behavior. 

That gap is what shows up as cost, latency, and reliability divergence in production agent systems.

Read the report: https://t.co/6thZVvLomR

🔥 Can DeepSeek R1 teach better than humans?

Our recent study shows that AI-generated reasoning chains can actually outperform human expert annotations in teaching smaller models.

When we fine-tuned models on GSM8K math problems, synthetic reasoning from DeepSeek R1 achieved 87% accuracy, significantly beating the 68% accuracy from human expert chains of thought.

By using larger models like DeepSeek R1 to teach smaller ones, we can maintain high performance while reducing costs – all without relying on expensive human annotations.

You can read the full blog here: https://t.co/tj2DnaycfV 

#deepseek #fireworksai

evidence.text:
We're thrilled to unveil FireAttention V4, our latest inference engine that achieves over 250 tokens/second on @nvidia's B200 GPUs using the groundbreaking NVFP4 precision format.

This advancement sets a new benchmark in latency, throughput, and cost-efficiency for LLM serving.

Here are some key highlights:

➡️ >250 tokens/second on DeepSeek V3 with FireAttention V4 on B200.

➡️ 3.5x throughput improvement over SGLang on H200.

➡️ Fully optimized backend with new TensorCore Gen 5 instructions.

Why NVFP4 on Blackwell?

Each GPU generation brings a precision format that maximizes performance: FP16 for Ampere, FP8 for Hopper, and now NVFP4 for Blackwell. NVFP4 offers double the FLOPs throughput and significantly reduces memory reads compared to other formats, making it the optimal choice for the B200 architecture.

Enterprise deployments using FireAttention V4 with FP4 are now available. Experience unparalleled speed and efficiency in your AI workloads.

Read the full blog post: https://t.co/CsyCR8opL2

evidence.author: dzhulgakov
evidence.rewritten_text: The author thinks the most important part of DeepSeek is its open weights, because the models can be downloaded and hosted anywhere instead of being tied to Chinese servers. They argue open weights prevent models from being secretly degraded, avoid restrictive proprietary terms, allow fine-tuning and adaptation, and make DeepSeek R1 a fully controllable reasoning model on par with O1.
evidence.text:
Open Weights is the most incredible part of DeepSeek. Many takes on X miss it out. Here’s what it means in simple terms:

🔒 “Your data is not going to China”. DeepSeek V3 and R1 models are just a bunch of numbers freely available for download. DeepSeek’s iOS App indeed runs on Chinese servers. But other app builders can choose where and how to host the model: run GPUs directly or use API providers in the geography of preference. @FireworksAI_HQ runs DeepSeek in US and EU and already powers many high-profile applications.

🧠 “Model won’t be lobotomized”. Many claim that proprietary model APIs suddenly getting dumber (whether real or not). With Open Weights, there’s no risk of it, and anyone can independently verify model generations from the hosted API.

📖 Worry-free usage. Open-source means no proprietary TOS limiting what you can do. Distill it to smaller models, use it as a grader for RL, or adapt it for specialized tasks. DeepSeek R1 stands out with top-notch reasoning on par with O1 - fully in your control.

🎨 Customization. Models are at the core of AI-first applications, and getting a delightful user experience requires more nuanced control than just prompting generic models. For open models, the whole toolkit of fine-tuning options is available to enhance the accuracy or style for the specific task. We are shipping more easy-to-use workflows soon 🚀

We are building @FireworksAI_HQ precisely because we believe in the power of open-source AI. Seeing the huge usage of DeepSeek inference & customization from our customers this past week reaffirms our mission. Thanks to Llama, Mistral, Qwen and now DeepSeek, the future of AI applications is as bright as ever. 🔥

evidence.author: dzhulgakov
evidence.rewritten_text: The author thinks Groq and Cerebras are effectively "SRAM-only" systems that maximize memory bandwidth by bypassing HBM, which makes them very fast for generation but with capacity tradeoffs, and says this is why there is no one-size-fits-all inference hardware choice.
evidence.text:
5/5 Finally - why do I call Cerebras and Groq “SRAM-only”? GPU systems from NVidia or AMD have several layers of memory hierarchy:

1) On-chip memory (SRAM, L1, registers): capacity 10-100 MB/chip, throughput 10-100 TB/s

2) High-bandwidth memory on the card (HBM): capacity 40-200 GB/chip, throughput 3-5 TB/s

3) Host memory (DDR): capacity 2+ TB/host, throughput ~100 GB/s

Groq and Cerebras bypass the HBM layer and max out SRAM and cross-chip interconnect instead. For Groq it’s many chips with optical interconnects. For Cerebras - many cores fit on one gigantic wafer. But the idea is the same: maximize memory bandwidth but sacrifice capacity. This creates superior generation speed at the cost of the drawbacks mentioned above.

Overall, as we often say @FireworksAI_HQ, there’s no “one-size-fit-all” inference: use cases vary widely on many dimensions. Having diverse hardware choices is really exciting for the broad adoption of LLM / GenAI tech everywhere.

However, Mixtral MoE is particularly susceptible to the “optimized throughout != optimized latency” caveat.

Even a modest batch size activates all 4 experts and slows down generation ~4x. While BS=1 is too costly.

evidence.author: FireworksAI_HQ
evidence.text:
Most people still think of LLMs as free form text generators. In practice, a huge number of real production workloads are actually classification problems. Things like intent detection, routing, moderation, or picking from a small set of labels.

The interesting part is that you don’t need a separate classification head or a custom model to do this. You can turn any generative model into a classifier by mapping each class to a token and letting the model’s next token distribution act as the class probability distribution.

It stays fully compatible with standard fine tuning. It keeps your inference stack simple. And the best part is that you can get well calibrated class probabilities for as little as a couple of dollars in compute. We used Qwen3 4B, trained with LoRA on Fireworks, and the total cost was around 2$.

Our latest blog breaks down the full workflow. How token based classes work. How calibration naturally emerges during training. And how you can serve high quality classifiers without changing model architecture at all.

If your team is building routing systems, moderation pipelines, intent detection, or any production workflow that depends on confidence scores, this is worth a read: https://t.co/MN5vQSu0k5

Groq is good at generation (but agree with you on the TCO and throughput points), but prompt processing is a different game, which I think Groq is weak comparing to Nvidia GPUs. It will be good to have all the benchmarking people to really test >8K prompt with 10:1 ratio and we can then see the fair and realistic comparison between Groq and Nvidia GPUs.

evidence.text:
In long agent loops, reasoning tokens get reused as context on every following turn. 

Shorter reasoning means smaller contexts downstream, faster generations, and fewer retries.

K2.7 Code reduces that overhead without giving up quality, which lowers the real cost per completed task.

evidence.author: dzhulgakov
evidence.rewritten_text: The author says an AI researcher’s answer to “What is group query attention?” is factually correct and detailed, but actually wrong because it implies GQA is sequence-sparse attention; they say GQA is multi-head attention with query projections shared between heads, and that the 405B model does not seem to know about GQA and hallucinates.
evidence.text:
Example: AI researcher question “What is group query attention?”

Claim: Factually correct, and detailed answer

Reality: The answer implies that GQA is some form of sequence-sparse attention. However, GQA is a form of multi-head attention where query projections are shared between heads (see our blog https://t.co/M7gsk0tidm). The 405B model doesn’t seem to know about GQA and hallucinates.

evidence.text: @ezyang Root cause is tight computation interdependence of SGD: you want many steps and each depends on the prev fully. More like HPC (akin to ODE solver) than MapReduce. Asynchronicity (like AdaDelay)/huge batch (close to epoch) turned out to give worse quality.

evidence.text:
Gemma 2 is looking really good and it's now live on @FireworksAI_HQ! The same day it was released.

I suspect something is off with @huggingface's implementation. In our evals, the Fireworks version gets 71.2% (base) / 72.2% (instruct) on MMLU, matching the announcement blog at 71.3%.

But HF gives only 58.8% (base), 69.6% (instruct).

The difference comes from sliding window attention (every other layer), but we haven't found the exact issue yet.

evidence.author: dzhulgakov
evidence.rewritten_text: The author says Cerebras’ new generation speed for Llama 70B is very impressive, reaching up to 450 generated tokens/s/user, about 5-9x faster than typical NVIDIA H100 speeds. But they are skeptical about the overall throughput and cost, estimating H100 may be 3-5x better on throughput per cost while Cerebras is 5-9x faster in raw generation speed.
evidence.text:
2/5 Throughput. Cerebras came out with really impressive generation speed today: up to 450 generated tokens/s/user for Llama 70B, about 5-9x faster than typical speeds on GPUs like NVidia H100. But what is the overall throughput, and hence the cost, of this system? While we don’t know pricing, let’s estimate it from public information.

My guess is that H100 is 3-5x more efficient (on throughput / cost), but CS is 5-9x faster in pure generation speed.

Technical details if you want to follow along.

The website (https://t.co/t9oHcxqUWU) quotes a 50x advantage over a single H100. Going off pure power usage (which is often the limiting factor), one CS chip is 23kW (O_O) compared to 0.7 kW for H100 - 33x difference. This gives us an estimated 33-50x cost difference.

One CS chip has only 44GB of memory, so loading Llama 70B in fp16 would require at least 4 chips. Cerebras’ announcement quotes a batch size of 1-100. My guess is that they run pipeline parallelism per layer (see picture), giving the batch size of 80. That’s 80*450 = 36 ktokens/s on 4 chips.

How does H100 do? 4xH100 running Llama 70B in fp16 at a sizeable batch size of 64 with short prompts gives about 50 tokens/s/user. That’s 50*64 = 3.2 ktokens/s on 4 chips - only 11x less than CS. Meanwhile, the power-based cost is 33-50x smaller per chip.

Disclaimer: the actual CS cost and setup is unknown, these are best guesses. I’d be cautious about public pricing as it might be subsidized. For H100s I don’t account for fp8 (1.5-2x faster/higher throughput) or speculative decoding (less efficient at high batches).

evidence.author: FireworksAI_HQ
evidence.text:
Until now, open-source LLMs forced a choice: show the chain of thought or call tools deterministically.

@Alibaba_Qwen 3’s new architecture does both in one pass, and keeps the reasoning block cleanly separated so your downstream code can ignore it or audit it.

Here’s what else stands out:

🧠 Explicit reasoning + JSON tool calls.
Qwen3 now streams <think>…</think> traces and the exact tool call in the same completion.

⚡ Transparent or turbo, your call.
Use /think tags or set reasoning_effort="none" to trade verbosity for speed.

🧩 128-expert MoE, 22B active.
Near-frontier quality, lower cost. Qwen3-235B-A22B activates just 8 experts per call and runs serverlessly on @FireworksAI_HQ.

🎯 Zero switching cost.
Fully OpenAI-compatible, just point your existing client to the Fireworks endpoint.

This model is fully Apache-2.0 licensed and available now on Fireworks AI

Check out the full blog here: https://t.co/Hdb1IUHRS7

👉 Fireworks - Qwen3 235B-A22B model: https://t.co/dMAPrSrioy

evidence.text:
🔥 @FireworksAI_HQ  Developer Cloud for @deepseek_ai  models 🐳 

Fireworks mission is to provide the best developer toolchain using open models for transparency, steerability, control, privacy, low latency and cost. Within one month, Fireworks launched a comprehensive AI developer cloud for Deepseek models. 

Here is a list of launched product features: 

👉 Launched Deepseek models on the same day of open weights release! 

👉 Latency and cost optimization: 
▶️ Fireworks continuously pushes top performance and cost efficiency with a special version of FireAttention and distributed inference engine optimized for Deepseek’s unique MLA, MTP, and wide MOE architecture. 
▶️ Controllable reasoning effort: we added shorter and better CoT for R1 via reasoning_effort = low

👉 Agentic development:
▶️ Agentic multi-modal workflow: we added vision capability to Deepseek v3 and R1 
▶️ Agentic tool use: we added function calling to Deepseek v3 so it can integrate easily with other tools and APIs for agent development
▶️ Constrained generation: we added JSON mode and Grammar mode to Deepseek v3 and R1 

👉 Model quality enhancement:
▶️ Additional Deepseek derivative models: launched @perplexity_ai R1-1776 with higher accuracy for deep research and many tuned Deepseek models in production

👉 Research reproduction:
▶️ Reinforcement learning verifiable reward with minimal label
▶️ Distillation: R1 doing better than human 

We have many more features to launch soon, including Deepseek SFT and RL tuning platform as part of the FireOptimizer [https://t.co/g5s5sJhNtc] stack. We will release many real world demos for you to get up to speed on the developer cloud of Deepseek. Stay tuned! 

Please comment below what your wish list is. I would love to hear from you.

evidence.text:
Still riding the energy from this week 🚀
We hosted a Fine-Tuning and Eval Protocol Workshop at Fireworks AI around a question we hear constantly: how do you actually customize open-source models in practice?

We went deep into supervised and reinforcement fine-tuning, but just as importantly, how to evaluate these systems once they move past demos. This wasn’t theory for its own sake. It was about building intuition for when fine-tuning helps, when it hurts, and why evals are the real guardrails in production.

We had builders and leaders from teams like Nuro, Alibaba Group, Adobe, eBay, Intuit, Coinbase, Pinterest, Reddit, Inc., Netflix, GEICO, Broadcom and more. The questions, debates, and hallway conversations were exactly why we love doing these in person.

Huge thanks to Lightspeed for hosting us, and to @pawelg  and @Aishwarya_Sri0  for leading the workshop. Watching people reason through eval design, reward signals, and model behavior in real time was energizing.

We’ll be doing more of these, both in person and virtual. If you’d like to join a future session, comment “Fireworks AI” and we’ll share updates.

evidence.author: FireworksAI_HQ
evidence.text:
Everyone is talking about @deepseek_ai's mHC (Manifold-Constrained Hyper-Connections) paper; let’s break it down in simple language.

What they’re trying to do: A model has one “main highway,” the residual stream, which carries information through all layers. DeepSeek splits that highway into a few parallel lanes so different lanes can carry different kinds of information, and the model can merge that traffic as it goes.

The problem: If every layer is allowed to freely “turn the knobs” on how much each lane is amplified, those knobs can compound over multiple layers. 
The result: Some lanes get too much traffic, others remain empty, and training becomes unstable.

DeepSeek added a simple rule: each layer can redistribute information across lanes but not amplify it overall, thereby conserving the information flow. To mix our metaphors slightly, think of it as a mixer board. You can move sound between channels, but you can’t secretly crank the overall volume. 

The non-obvious win: This rule holds even when stacking many layers. Instead of small mistakes that add up and explode, you get stable mixing all the way down, while still benefiting from multiple lanes.

What you get in practice: training becomes much more predictable, and quality improves across benchmarks (final loss reduction of 0.021 vs the baseline on a 27B LLM).

Why it matters for real systems: This kind of multi-lane design can be expensive to run. DeepSeek also did engineering work on custom kernels and a smarter backward pass, so it’s only ~7 percent slower to train with four lanes, which is surprisingly practical.

Net: More expressive residual connections, without the usual instability, and the overhead is small enough to actually use.

This is really impressive research. We’re excited to see the next generation of models built on top of this architecture. In the meantime, if you’re ready to try today’s DeepSeek models (and other frontier open weight models), you can get started with one click. You can run them on the Fireworks Inference Cloud, which delivers open-source models at blazing speed, optimized for your use case, and scaled globally: https://t.co/e8syugMkdp

Today, we’re launching Voice Agent Platform (Beta) on @FireworksAI_HQ   — a developer-first stack to build interactive, production-grade voice agents.

Sub-500ms latency: Our proprietary ASR, TTS, and LLMs are co-located and optimized for parallel, real-time performance.

If you’re building production-grade AI systems, function calling is fundamental.

At Fireworks AI, we’ve been working with teams pushing LLMs from text generation toward agentic behavior- systems that plan, act, and reason with structured tools. Function calling is at the heart of this shift.

In our latest blog, we go deep on:

→ Designing function-calling workflows using the Fireworks AI SDK

→ How pairwise evaluation + function execution unlock robust RAG and decision systems

→ Why Model Context Protocol (MCP) matters, and how it solves the N×M integration problem across LLMs and tools

→ How function calling scales beyond a single API call into multi-step orchestration for real-world applications

This is the core infrastructure for building LLM-native applications, and it’s how teams are moving from prototypes to autonomous agents in prod.

evidence.author: dzhulgakov
evidence.rewritten_text: The author says they tried the exact same settings with temp=0 and got infinite looping, then tried again and got the correct answer versus looping only 50% of the time. They believe temp=0 is not fully deterministic in the engine, that cuBLAS matmuls are not numerically stable, and that tiny numerical differences at the edge of what 405B can do can send the model off the rails; they also criticize cherry-picking such cases for marketing purposes.
evidence.text:
I tried the exact same settings (temp=0) this morning and got infinite looping (see the video in the original thread). I tried it now and I get the correct answer vs looping 50% of the time.

https://t.co/QEHc7Tmghl

It looks like temp=0 is not fully deterministic in your engine (we saw it too). It's not too surprising, even cuBLAS matmuls aren't numerically stable. Depending on the batch size the implementation algorithm can be vastly different. And batch size changes depending on the server load.

The important point is that these examples are at the edge of what 405B can do and tiny numerical differences can send the model off the rails. We can all do better than cherry-picking such cases for marketing purposes.

evidence.author: FireworksAI_HQ
evidence.text:
Faster Reasoning– Now Live with DeepSeek-R1 on Fireworks AI! 🔥

We’re expanding reasoning_effort controls with the latest DeepSeek-R1 model, allowing developers to optimize computational efficiency while maintaining strong task performance.

𝗪𝗵𝗮𝘁’𝘀 𝗡𝗲𝘄?

✔️ reasoning_effort = low: Limits Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning to 40% of full length, improving response times for latency-sensitive applications.
✔️ Benchmark Performance: Achieves 63% accuracy on AIME 2024 math competition problems, outperforming o1-mini_low (60%).
✔️ Customizable Effort Levels: Developers can fine-tune reasoning depth by setting reasoning_effort = [integer < 20,000], directly controlling computational limits.

𝗜𝗺𝗽𝗹𝗲𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗗𝗲𝘁𝗮𝗶𝗹𝘀

→ Faster, Focused Outputs: Shorter CoT paths reduce unnecessary reasoning overhead.
→ Hard Cutoff for CoT: The model auto-terminates reasoning with an </think> token at the defined effort limit.
→Minimized Unproductive Loops: Conditional logprob adjustments discourage unnecessary deliberation.
→Backward Compatible: Existing workflows using reasoning_effort = medium remain unaffected.

𝗪𝗵𝘆 𝗨𝘀𝗲 𝗿𝗲𝗮𝘀𝗼𝗻𝗶𝗻𝗴_𝗲𝗳𝗳𝗼𝗿𝘁 = 𝗹𝗼𝘄?

↳ Optimized for latency-sensitive, cost-aware applications.
↳ Reduces unnecessary processing while maintaining competitive accuracy.
↳ Streamlined integration—no prompt tuning required, just a single parameter update.

𝗪𝗵𝗮𝘁’𝘀 𝗡𝗲𝘅𝘁?

We’re exploring reasoning_effort = high for applications requiring extended reasoning depth. More updates coming soon.

𝗚𝗲𝘁 𝗦𝘁𝗮𝗿𝘁𝗲𝗱 ↓

Set reasoning_effort in your API call:

response = https://t.co/JoFFm7Wpfc.completions.create(
model="fireworks/deepseek-r1",
reasoning_effort="low"
)

--------

Try DeepSeek R1 models on Fireworks AI: https://t.co/Aef6IWCZZd

evidence.author: FireworksAI_HQ
evidence.text:
How do you assign rewards in tasks where “correctness” is subjective- like style, coherence, or creativity?

Traditional reward functions struggle in open-ended domains. But in our latest blog, we show how using a stronger LLM as a judge offers a powerful alternative.

With the Fireworks AI Reinforcement Finetuning (RFT) API, we fine-tuned a Qwen2.5 32B base model for creative writing tasks- achieving a 93.8% win rate against the original model on the Arena Hard Auto dataset.

🧠 Instead of rule-based metrics, we used a model-as-a-judge to assign rewards through pairwise comparison, evaluating which response better reflects nuanced human-like reasoning.

🚀 Deployed and scaled via the Fireworks Python SDK, the setup also integrates with the open-source Qwen3 235B model hosted on Fireworks.

This shows how LLM-as-a-judge strategies can help train models in domains where traditional supervision fails.

📖 Read the full breakdown here: https://t.co/pPGMuqJwh5

evidence.text:
2/ Own your stack, build application tuned model, avoid lock-in

You can keep swapping in new open base models as the frontier moves, reusing the same evaluator + training pipeline instead of being tied to one closed provider. Fireworks RFT standardizes the following primitives:

Evaluator (Goal)
👉 Choose your favorite coding language to score an agent trajectory.
👉 Encodes “good” for your use case (accuracy, structure, latency, tool usage, etc).

Rollout (Action)
👉 One full run of your multi-turn agent in your environment.
👉 Uses your real tools, APIs, internal systems, and context.
👉 Produces a trajectory + trace that’s fed into training.

Training Loop (Update)
👉 Fireworks runs the RL training loop:
       Generate rollouts → score with evaluator → update model weights.

evidence.author: FireworksAI_HQ
evidence.text:
Your AI benchmark might be lying to you!
We ran a test where an AI-generated image scored 93.3% on a standard benchmark. It checked all 15 boxes on the evaluation list. Technically perfect!

But when you look at the image, it’s clear something’s off.
It doesn’t resemble the concept it was supposed to depict.
It’s visually awkward.
It’s not usable.

That’s the problem with many benchmark systems today—they measure compliance, not quality.

At Fireworks AI, we built Eval Protocol (EP) to change that.
Instead of rigid checklists, EP applies a flexible, human-centered rubric based on 5 key dimensions:
→ Intent Matching
→ Content Recognizability
→ Spatial Design
→ UX Clarity
→ Visual Coherence

That same image, evaluated under EP, scored just 39%—a much more accurate reflection of its actual quality. And with a hybrid evaluation (70% human preference + 30% checklist), it landed at 66%.

Why it matters:
→ Human intuition can be codified
→ Better benchmarks lead to better models
→ Evaluation should reflect how people actually judge outputs—not just whether requirements were met

If your benchmarks are too easy to game, they’re not benchmarks. They’re blind spots.
📖 Read the full post here: https://t.co/88ee31cNa8

evidence.author: FireworksAI_HQ
evidence.text:
Fine-tuning has quietly become one of the most important levers in making large language models enterprise-ready.

While base models like Kimi K2, Qwen 3, or DeepSeek v3 show remarkable generalization, they often fall short when precision, compliance, or verifiable outputs are non-negotiable. That’s where fine-tuning steps in to bridge the gap between broad capability and domain-specific reliability.

In our latest Fireworks AI deep-dive, we explore what fine-tuning really means and how it differs from pre-training, when it makes sense to fine-tune instead of relying on RAG or prompt engineering, and the best practices across data curation, model selection, and training stability. We also dive into post-training alignment strategies such as SFT, DPO, and RLVR, along with real-world applications across industries like healthcare, finance, legal, customer support, and engineering.

If you’re an AI engineer, researcher, or product leader building production-grade systems, this guide will help you make informed, repeatable, and evaluation-driven decisions.

Read the full deep-dive here: https://t.co/sbDwDIu25A

evidence.author: lqiao
evidence.text:
Creative UX vs Data Gravity, which wins the commercial market in the Gen AI application race? 

Creative UX requires a ground-up product design natively built on Gen AI tech. Only then, founders can push the boundaries to do what is not possible before. To do it well, founders need to have deep insights of Gen AI model capability across a broad spectrum of models with a high velocity of UX experimentation speed bundled with model testing and tuning. 

Data gravity argues for building product features as add-ons using Gen AI. There are so many established companies owning all kinds of data, from enterprise internal documents, CRM, workplace communication, internal codebase, logs, BI, to call recordings for GTM, etc. Each of these companies owning data and data-driven applications can enhance their products through Gen AI features and close the UX loop within current product. 

Enterprise search is one example such race will get heated very fast. Who can get both right will definitely be the winner, but usually it's one vs another.

evidence.author: FireworksAI_HQ
evidence.text:
Don’t have real-world data for fine-tuning? No worries, we got you. 

At Fireworks AI, we’ve built a fully automated Synthetic Data Pipeline that generates high-quality datasets, evaluates model outputs with LLM-as-a-judge, and iteratively fine-tunes models using reinforcement loops, all without touching proprietary or sensitive data.

🔍 Here's a breakdown of what makes it powerful:

→ LLM-Orchestrated Config Generation: From a plain-text task prompt, we auto-generate YAML configs with input/output schema, diversity filters, and evaluation metrics.

→ Streaming Synthetic Generation: Our pipeline emits JSON-formatted examples with prompt-response-metadata structure, complete with per-row quality metrics and job-level visibility.

→ Automated Fine-Tuning + Eval: Multiple rounds of SFT and RFT use DeepSeek R1 as an evaluator to guide learning. Low-performing outputs are clustered to identify knowledge gaps, which are fed back to regenerate better data.

→ Closed-Loop Optimization: The system updates itself, expanding label schemas, enriching prompts, or even adjusting temperature/top‑p settings for creative vs. deterministic output.

→ Compliance-First by Design: No PII, no client data. Just high-quality task-specific synthetic datasets that match or exceed the performance of traditional pipelines.

📈 Results? On one internal benchmark:
- SFT with curated data: 79% accuracy (needs real data)
- RFT with synthetic data: 73% accuracy (zero real data)

If you're looking to prototype fine-tuned models without data access friction, this pipeline is now available to test, adapt, and scale: https://t.co/uuh2ulayPq

evidence.author: FireworksAI_HQ
evidence.text:
Fine-tuning isn’t just a feature- it’s foundational to building differentiated, production-ready models.

At Fireworks AI, Supervised Fine-Tuning V2 (SFT V2) has been the backbone for teams fine-tuning open models at scale, across agentic workflows, retrieval systems, and domain-specific applications.

What makes SFT V2 effective in production:

→ Training efficiency at scale
Fine-tune large models like Qwen-72B on 1M tokens in under 10 minutes with Turbo mode. Iterate rapidly across versions without GPU bottlenecks.

→ Support for long-context use cases
Train with sequences up to 131K tokens, enabling document agents, multi-hop reasoning, and memory-intensive planning.

→ Multi-LoRA deployment
Serve multiple fine-tuned variants on a single base model with minimal latency- ideal for A/B testing and routing by context.

→ Quantization-Aware Training (QAT)
Support for FP4 and FP8 QAT delivers inference efficiency without compromising output quality.

→ Function-calling and multi-turn supervision
Fine-tune agents that reason, call tools, and handle structured outputs across multiple turns, with grounded, reproducible behavior.

SFT V2 is built for engineers who care about control, quality, and scale. It’s not new—but it’s become essential.

📖 Learn more: https://t.co/2c0iJ6VJsV

evidence.author: FireworksAI_HQ
evidence.text:
Fine-tuning has quietly become one of the most important levers in making large language models enterprise-ready.

While base models like Kimi K2, Qwen 3, or DeepSeek v3 show remarkable generalization, they often fall short when precision, compliance, or verifiable outputs are non-negotiable. That’s where fine-tuning steps in to bridge the gap between broad capability and domain-specific reliability.

In our latest Fireworks AI deep-dive, we explore what fine-tuning really means and how it differs from pre-training, when it makes sense to fine-tune instead of relying on RAG or prompt engineering, and the best practices across data curation, model selection, and training stability. We also dive into post-training alignment strategies such as SFT, DPO, and RLVR, along with real-world applications across industries like healthcare, finance, legal, customer support, and engineering.

If you’re an AI engineer, researcher, or product leader building production-grade systems, this guide will help you make informed, repeatable, and evaluation-driven decisions.

Read the full deep-dive here: https://t.co/DomMAzZHyS

evidence.author: FireworksAI_HQ
evidence.text:
There’s been some really interesting work recently connecting DPO and GRPO.
The “It Takes Two: Your GRPO Is Secretly DPO” paper made an important point: under simple assumptions, GRPO and DPO aren’t that different. Both are learning by contrast.

If you run DPO repeatedly on fresh preference data, it stops being a one-time tuning trick and starts looking a lot like reinforcement learning in practice.

Production systems already generate comparisons.
Users already express preference, explicitly or implicitly.
Turn that into DPO pairs and retrain on a cadence.

You don’t need a complex online RL loop, or fragile training orchestration, you just retrain regularly on real preference data, and the model gets better over time while staying stable. For many real-world systems, this approach scales better.

At Fireworks AI, we’re seeing teams use DPO this way not because it’s easier than RL, but because it fits how production systems actually evolve, incrementally, reliably, and with fast feedback loops. https://t.co/lYJPY43bHn

evidence.text:
When you optimize for the wrong rewards, models learn the wrong behavior.

In our Reward Hacking tutorial, we walk through how to design structured, multi-signal rewards to guide LLMs toward trustworthy, relevant outputs- while avoiding shortcuts that lead to poor generations.

This example highlights how to:
→ Use ROUGE-L and bullet recall for content relevance
→ Apply length gates to prevent verbose or bloated responses
→ Integrate fluency rewards for polished, readable output
→ Combine these signals in reinforcement fine-tuning (RFT) workflows

Whether you're building summarization agents, recap tools, or evaluation pipelines, getting the reward design right is critical.

📖 Explore the full guide: https://t.co/fuEBJAWeHW

evidence.text:
Not every LLM task needs to happen in real time. Sometimes, you just want to run a ton of calls- cheaply, reliably, and without hitting rate limits.

That’s why we built the Fireworks Batch API.

It’s the tool we wished we had when we were running prompt evaluations, synthetic data generation, and tool-call tests across thousands of inputs. Instead of wrangling infrastructure or hitting throttles, you just drop in a JSONL file and go.
✅ No rate limits
✅ Up to 50% cheaper than real-time calls, with an additional stacked cached prompt token discount
✅ 24-hour guaranteed turnaround (often way faster)
✅ Works across 1,000+ models—including your own fine-tunes
Whether you're experimenting at scale or shipping pipelines to production, Batch API gives you speed, scale, and simplicity, without the infra burden.

📖 Find the detailed Batch API blog here: https://t.co/sLZkGua1iI

evidence.author: FireworksAI_HQ
evidence.text:
Ever tried teaching an AI what good creative writing looks like?

It’s like explaining why one poem moves you and another doesn’t - there’s no checklist for nuance.

At Fireworks, we tackled this by skipping hardcoded rules and letting a smarter model act as the judge.

Think of it like a senior editor guiding a junior writer.

With this model-as-judge approach, our fine-tuned model hit 93.8% win rate over the base in creative tasks. And the gains aren’t limited to writing, we’re seeing similar boosts in math and coding tasks too.

The real breakthrough? Making reinforcement learning more intuitive and scalable. No more wrestling with brittle reward functions that miss the nuance.

Read how it works: https://t.co/boO5NFqdmm

Are you using LLM-as-a-Judge in your workflow? If so, what use-case are you building?

evidence.author: FireworksAI_HQ
evidence.text:
Modern AI stacks are evolving faster than the models they run on.
Teams are now mixing OpenAI, Anthropic, and open-source models, sometimes even running them locally for privacy, latency, or cost reasons. But migrating between models shouldn’t mean rebuilding your entire app.

In our latest post, we share how to use Fireworks Eval Protocol with Ollama to keep your evaluation workflow intact while swapping model backends effortlessly. The setup lets you compare OpenAI and OSS models head-to-head using the same evaluation harness, so your results stay consistent, and your code stays clean.

We walk through two examples: agent evaluation on the Chinook dataset and LLM-judge scoring from Langfuse traces.

One surprising result: qwen3:8b outperformed gpt-4o-mini, making it a strong option for local deployment.

If you’re thinking about migrating to open models without losing rigor or observability, this one’s worth a read: https://t.co/Vfvu5HOof7

Cresta transformed its contact center operations by integrating Fireworks AI into its Knowledge Assist platform. Using enterprise RAG, Fireworks AI retrieves and synthesizes information from multiple knowledge sources in real time, delivering context-aware guidance for agents through a single intelligent access point.
The result? Agents get the right information at the right time, boosting productivity and customer satisfaction.

Key Outcomes:
→ 100X cost reduction through multi-LoRA fine-tuning → enterprise-scale efficiency
→ Faster, context-aware agent guidance → shorter handle times and higher resolution rates
→ Reliable, low-latency performance under high-volume operations → production-ready at scale

Read the full Cresta case study: https://t.co/CsMLq4TBP4

evidence.author: dzhulgakov
evidence.rewritten_text: The author highlights new tools for training ranking and recommendation models in PyTorch, saying they can scale to trillions of parameters and hundreds of GPUs, and that rec models are sparse with many nn.EmbeddingBags so TorchRec provides special parallelism primitives.
evidence.text:
Latest goodies for training ranking/recommendation models in PyTorch. Scaling to trillions of params and 100s of GPUs.

Compared to "trillions" in LMs, rec models are "sparse" with a lot of nn.EmbeddingBags. Thus special parallelism primitives that TorchRec provides.
